stem: A hospital uses a skin tuberculin test to screen for tuberculosis. The test has a sensitivity of 92% and a specificity of 97%. The prevalence of tuberculosis in the patient population is estimated to be between 1.7% and 2.9%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has tuberculosis? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(tuberculosis | positive test) ranges from 0.347 to 0.478 as prevalence varies from 1.7% to 2.9%. Point estimate at 2.3% prevalence: 0.419.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.03.
The confidence interval [0.347, 0.478] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (92%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
